title: Emergency Guide: Cat Unable to Stand on Back Legs
meta_desc: If your cat cannot stand on its back legs, it is a high-priority emergency. Learn about potential causes like FATE and why immediate care is vital.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden hind limb paralysis in cats is a life-threatening medical emergency often caused by blood clots or spinal injuries. Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if your cat cannot use their back legs or if their limbs feel cold to the touch.

What does it mean if my cat suddenly loses the use of their back legs?

  • The cat may drag their rear limbs or show a sudden lack of coordination (ataxia).
  • Legs may appear limp and feel noticeably cold to the touch.
  • This crisis is often caused by a blood clot, spinal injury, or a severe neurological event.

Is my cat's sudden hind limb paralysis a medical emergency?

  • Yes, this is a high-priority emergency requiring immediate contact with an emergency veterinarian.
  • Conditions like Feline Aortic Thromboembolism (FATE), or "Saddle Thrombus," are extremely painful and life-threatening.
  • Rapid intervention is critical to address vascular or neurological deficits and manage pain.

How can photos and videos help a veterinarian triage my cat's condition?

  • Record a brief video of your cat's attempts to move to help the medical team assess the severity of the deficit.
  • Provide a video because cats often hide symptoms or stop moving due to stress once they arrive at the clinic.
  • Take a clear photo of your cat's paw pads so the vet can check for discoloration, which indicates a lack of blood flow.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

When a cat presents with an inability to stand on its back legs, potential causes include thromboembolism secondary to underlying diseases such as cardiomyopathy, or diseases of the spinal column and cord, encompassing congenital disorders, degenerative diseases, inflammatory or infectious diseases, neoplasia, nutritional diseases, trauma, toxic disorders, and vascular diseases. Thromboembolism management involves analgesics, nursing care, treatment of underlying disease, and anticoagulants like unfractionated or low-molecular-weight heparin to reduce further thrombus formation; neurologic deficits may take weeks to months to resolve, and the long-term prognosis is guarded due to the risk of recurrence. Spinal cord diseases may present with posterior paresis or paralysis and urinary and fecal incontinence.

Chapter: Cardiology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1245)

faqs.question: Is my cat in pain if they can't move their legs?
faqs.answer: Yes, sudden hind limb paralysis, especially when caused by a blood clot, is considered one of the most painful conditions a cat can experience. They may vocalize loudly or breathe with an open mouth.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of back leg weakness?
faqs.answer: The most common causes include blood clots (thromboembolism), spinal cord injuries, slipped discs, or extreme cases of diabetic neuropathy.

faqs.question: What should I do while transporting my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat as calm and still as possible. Use a sturdy carrier lined with soft towels and avoid touching the back legs, as the area may be very sensitive or painful.
